title: One-share-one-vote default in companies legislation
description: National companies legislation enforces one-share-one-vote as the default for private companies, blocking multi-class share structures with differentiated voting, dividend, or liquidation rights. Founders are forced to incorporate parent companies in Delaware, Mauritius, or Singapore to access standard venture governance.
surgicalFix: Insert an exemption permitting labelled startups to issue multi-class share structures with appropriate disclosure, leaving the default rule intact for non-labelled companies.

title: SAFEs and convertible notes not recognised as securities
description: Securities frameworks across most enacted-act countries either classify SAFEs and convertible notes as debt (Nigeria SEC Rules 2013, Tunisia BCT Feb-2020 memo) or fail to recognise them at all (OHADA AUSCGIE, Ethiopia Securities Proclamation). This forces early-stage rounds offshore and distorts accounting and tax treatment.
surgicalFix: Amend the relevant securities statute or regulator instruction to recognise SAFEs and convertible notes as valid equity-linked securities when issued by a labelled startup; treat them as equity for accounting, tax, and prudential purposes.

title: No standardised ESOP / equity-compensation framework
description: Labor and tax codes across all 6 enacted-act countries either prohibit or fail to support employee stock-option plans with tax obligations deferred to liquidity. Tunisia's IRPP Article 38 taxes equity grants as immediate wages on theoretical values; Nigeria's Labor Act has no equity-comp provisions; OHADA states lack ESOP treatment in SYSCOHADA accounting. The result: African startups can't compete with offshore-domiciled peers for senior talent.
surgicalFix: Amend the labor and personal-income-tax codes to defer taxation of equity grants in labelled startups until a liquidity event (sale, IPO, or secondary). Add standardised vesting and cliff provisions to companies legislation.

title: Forex authorisation layered on top of Startup Act forex grants
description: Startup acts grant labelled companies the right to operate foreign-currency accounts (Tunisia Article 17, Nigeria forex provisions, DRC Article 32), but underlying foreign-exchange codes still require central-bank authorisation for the same accounts (Tunisia FX Code Article 6, CBN FX Manual Section 6, DRC FX Regulations Article 70, BCEAO 09/2010). The Startup Act grant becomes a paper right.
surgicalFix: Amend the foreign-exchange code or regulator manual to auto-recognise the Startup Act forex grant. For digital-service exports, replace daily/30-day repatriation requirements with quarterly reporting.

title: Tax benefits require separate verification after labelling
description: All 6 enacted-act countries promise tax holidays, but none apply them automatically on labelling. Senegal requires separate DGID verification (45-60 days vs the 30-day statutory limit). Nigeria's Schedule 3 incentives aren't integrated into the federal tax system. Tunisia, Ivory Coast, and DRC have similar gaps. Founders wait months for benefits they're already legally entitled to.
surgicalFix: Amend the relevant tax code to include a single clause: 'Tax benefits under [the Startup Act] shall apply automatically from the date of label issuance without requiring separate application or verification by [the tax authority].' Build API integration between the labelling portal and the tax authority.

title: No mutual recognition of startup labels across borders
description: Despite shared REC membership, no enacted startup act recognises another country's labelling. A company labelled in Senegal must reapply in Ivory Coast despite both being ECOWAS, OHADA, and UEMOA members with nearly identical qualification criteria. Wave expanding Senegal → Ivory Coast restarts the regulatory process entirely. The Ghana–Rwanda Fintech Passport (Feb 2025) is the only operational counter-example.
surgicalFix: Bilateral mutual-recognition agreement under existing REC treaty powers (ECOWAS Article 59 + Protocol on Trade in Services 2019; EAC Common Market Protocol Articles 13-14; OHADA harmonisation; AMU equivalents). Sector-first pilots — fintech, e-commerce, logistics — before economy-wide.

title: OHADA AUSCGIE rigidity blocks startup-friendly corporate forms
description: OHADA's Uniform Act on Commercial Companies (AUSCGIE) limits OHADA-state companies to SARL, SA, and SAS forms (Article 6), with Articles 309-314 imposing fixed capital and governance requirements. Article 39 establishes a default one-share-one-vote rule. Articles 233-246 of the Uniform Act on Secured Transactions don't recognise SAFEs or convertible notes. National startup acts within OHADA cannot unilaterally fix any of this — it requires regional consensus across all 17 member states.
surgicalFix: Amend OHADA AUSCGIE Articles 5 and 309-314 to create a Société Panafricaine d'Innovation (SAS-I) — a modular vehicle with multi-class shares, flexible governance, and standardised convertible-instrument recognition. This is a regional, not national, intervention.

title: Foreign-worker quotas constrain technical talent
description: Tunisia's labor law caps foreign personnel at 30%, reverting to 10% after three years. Senegal imposes a 20% quota. Both conflict with the talent strategy of digital ventures recruiting AI engineers, ML specialists, and senior product talent who are scarce locally.
surgicalFix: Amend the labor code to raise the cap to 50% for labelled startups, with sunset clauses tied to local-talent training commitments. Recognise non-resident technical specialists under remote-work arrangements.

title: Government agencies operate without integration
description: Startup labelling, tax administration, corporate registry, and central-bank verification operate on disconnected systems. Tunisia's portal is fully operational but isolated; Nigeria's NCDIE doesn't share data with FIRS or CAC; Senegal's DER/FJ, DGID, and Customs require separate submissions; DRC's CNPE digital portal is still in development. Founders submit the same information 4-6 times.
surgicalFix: Deploy API connections between labelling portal, tax authority, corporate registry, and central bank. Single-submission model: the startup files once with the labelling portal; downstream systems consume the data automatically.

title: Patent and tech-transfer processing creates multi-month delays
description: Tunisia's INNORPI takes 24 months for patent support despite Startup Act Article 12 promising expedited processing. Nigeria's NOTAP adds 2-6 months to technology-transfer arrangements. These delays are particularly painful for AI and biotech ventures racing competitors.
surgicalFix: Exempt labelled startups from standard NOTAP review for intra-group IP licensing. Mandate expedited patent processing (under 6 months) for labelled startups, backed by a service-level commitment.

title: Data-protection frameworks diverge across borders
description: ECOWAS adopted a binding Supplementary Act on Personal Data Protection (2010), but national implementation is uneven. Senegal's law misaligns with the Supplementary Act; Nigeria's NDPR and Ghana's DPA have different consent and breach-notification rules. Platform startups operating across borders maintain contradictory compliance systems.
surgicalFix: Bring national data-protection laws into alignment with the ECOWAS Supplementary Act (in West Africa) and the EAC Data Protection Bill (in East Africa). Mutual recognition of data-adequacy determinations between RECs.

title: Pension funds restricted from investing in startups
description: Despite 35% of African startup investors now being Africa-based, pension funds remain largely unable to invest in startups due to regulatory restrictions on alternative assets. Ghana mandates pension funds allocate 5% to startups by 2026 — outpacing Ghana's own startup act. Nigeria's PenCom rules and Senegal's equivalent constrain institutional capital flow.
surgicalFix: Amend pension-fund regulations to permit allocations to licensed startup funds (not direct startup equity) with prudential guardrails. Ghana's 5%-by-2026 mandate is the template.
